narrative: As a white male applicant with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 37, slightly edging out the average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many law school hopefuls would envy. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 45.1%, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection of your application. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—far below the average of 5.53—standing out as the most influential, as it strongly signaled to the model a higher chance of bar exam struggles despite your earlier strengths. In contrast, your outstanding undergraduate GPA pushed significantly in the opposite direction, suggesting you'd likely pass and helping to offset some concerns. Your family's upper-middle income level also worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward success compared to lower-income peers who often face steeper odds. While your Year 1 decile of 1 shows early promise, it wasn't as pivotal here, and elements like your full-time status fell in line with typical successful applicants. Overall, your profile compares favorably to averages in GPA, LSAT, and income, but that lower Year 3 performance pulled the prediction downward more than your strengths could fully counterbalance for this model. To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your later-year rankings, as schools value consistent upward trajectories for bar success. You might also gather letters from professors highlighting your Year 3 resilience or explore programs with holistic reviews that weigh your high GPA more heavily. Reapplying after addressing that decile gap could flip the script, given how close your 45.1% risk estimate was to approval territory. With your solid baseline, persistence will pay off—many applicants in similar spots turn rejections into acceptances by focusing on these fixable areas.
